title: Quiz: Option Greeks and Risk Management
description: Test your understanding of option Greeks and their applications

questions.question: What is the delta of a deep in-the-money call option?
questions.explanation: Deep ITM calls have delta close to 1, meaning they move almost dollar-for-dollar with the underlying stock. They behave almost like owning the stock itself.

questions.question: When is gamma highest for an option?

options.text: Gamma is constant across all strikes

questions.explanation: Gamma is highest for ATM options near expiration. This is when delta changes most rapidly as the underlying moves, creating significant gamma risk for option sellers.

questions.question: What does negative theta mean for a long option position?

options.text: The option gains value over time
The option loses value over time

questions.explanation: Negative theta means the option loses value as time passes, all else equal. This 'time decay' accelerates as expiration approaches, particularly for ATM options.

questions.question: To delta hedge a short call position, what should you do?
questions.explanation: A short call has negative delta exposure (you lose when stock rises). To hedge, buy shares (positive delta) equal to the absolute delta times contract size. This creates a delta-neutral position.

questions.question: Vega is positive for both long calls and long puts.
questions.explanation: True. Both calls and puts increase in value when volatility rises. Long option positions are 'long vega' - they benefit from volatility increases because higher volatility means greater probability of favorable price movements.

questions.question: What relationship exists between call delta and put delta for the same strike and expiry?
questions.explanation: Put delta = Call delta - 1. If an ATM call has delta of 0.5, the corresponding put has delta of -0.5. This relationship comes from put-call parity.

questions.question: Which Greek measures the sensitivity to a 1% change in implied volatility?
questions.explanation: Vega measures how much the option price changes for a 1% (1 percentage point) change in implied volatility. It's typically quoted as the dollar change per 1% vol move.

questions.question: Which statements about gamma are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Gamma is the same for calls and puts at the same strike
Short gamma positions lose money when the market moves significantly
Gamma is always positive for long options
Gamma decreases as time to expiration increases

questions.explanation: Gamma is identical for calls and puts at the same strike. Long options have positive gamma. Short gamma positions (option sellers) lose when markets move sharply because they must buy high/sell low when rehedging.
